title: Chapter 26: The Uncle
content: The front door opens before I reach it.

Henry Wingrave stands in the foyer like a man who has been carved from granite and then dressed in a rumpled greatcoat. Water drips from the hem onto the tile. His hair, silver at the temples and pressed flat by the rain, catches the dim light from the chandelier above. He carries a leather folio under one arm. No luggage. No assistant. Whatever he left in London, he left in a hurry.
He looks at me the way a surgeon looks at a tumor.
"You must be Maeve," he says.

I smell the rain on him from three feet away — cold and mineral, the particular London rain that carries diesel and wet pavement in its memory. His coat is soaked through at the shoulders. He didn't wait for a car from the regional airport. He drove himself, or had someone drive him straight here through the storm.
"You saw the stream," I say.
"I saw enough." He steps inside and closes the door behind him. The sound is heavy, final, like a file drawer shutting. "Where are the children?"
"Asleep." I almost add *I think*, but I don't. Not in front of two million people.

Henry glances around the foyer with an appraising eye — the water-stained ceiling, the cracked plaster, the security camera in the corner with its red indicator light burning steady. He doesn't know it's broadcasting. He doesn't know that as we stand here, in this drafty entry hall that smells of damp stone and old wood polish, an audience the size of a small nation is watching him size me up.
"Let's sit," he says. It is not a request.

He moves past me into the drawing room. I follow, my bare feet cold against the floor. I left my shoes somewhere — in the control room, maybe, or the kitchen. I can't remember. My phone is in my hand, the stream dashboard open, the viewer count a steady amber glow at the top of the screen. 2,114,387. Henry settles into one of the wingback chairs and opens the leather folio on his knee. Inside: a cream-colored document, multi-paged, clipped at the corner. Beside it, a single sheet of paper — a check, already filled out.
He doesn't offer me the facing chair. I sit anyway.

"You've caused quite a mess," he says. His voice is the kind of reasonable that makes unreasonable things sound inevitable. He speaks like a man who has delivered this speech before, many times, to many people who caused many messes. The words come out smooth, warm almost, like it's a kindness.
"I've told the truth," I say.

Henry smiles. Not with his eyes. "You've told *your* truth. That's not the same thing." He removes the check from the folio and places it on the table between us, face up. I read the number. Seven figures. The kind of money that changes a life — or ends one, depending on how you look at it.

"Sign an NDA. Leave tonight." He taps the multi-page document. "Non-disparagement, non-disclosure, liquidated damages clause. Standard. Your solicitor — if you have one — can review it, but I think you'll find the terms generous. You walk away. This ends."

The paper of the NDA looks heavy, expensive. The kind of paper that says *this document was drafted by people who bill eight hundred pounds an hour.* I can feel the chill of my phone screen against my palm. The viewer count has climbed to 2,203,501.

I reach across the table. Henry's eyes track my hand. I pick up the check, hold it for a moment — feel the weight of the paper, the raised print of the account number, the water stain where a droplet from Henry's sleeve has made the ink bleed at the corner — and set it back down.
"I'm not here for money."
Henry leans back. Something flickers across his face — not surprise, exactly. A recalculation. The adjustment of a man who brought one playbook and is discovering it might be the wrong one.
"Everyone's here for money, Maeve."
"Not everyone."

"That's a child's argument." He steeples his fingers. "Let me be plain. I've been managing Peter's messes since before you were born. The Game is not new to me. The problems it creates are not novel. The solution is always the same."
The same. Every governess. Every one of them took the check.

I lift my phone. Turn the screen toward him. The dashboard fills with light — the viewer counter, the chat scrolling in real-time, the small preview window showing two figures in a drawing room, one of them now looking at a glowing rectangle in the other's hand.
"Two million people are watching you try to buy my silence," I say.
Henry's face changes.

It is a subtle thing — the kind of shift that most people would miss. But I am looking for it, and I see it: the tightening around his eyes, the fractional straightening of his spine. For the first time since he walked through that door, Henry Wingrave is not in control of the narrative. The full weight of what I have done settles across his shoulders. Two million witnesses. No legal team in the world can serve an NDA to two million people.
He looks at the camera in the corner. Then back at me. Then at the camera again.
"Is that—" he begins.
"The security system is still streaming. Yes. Everything since you walked through the door. The check. The NDA. All of it."

A long silence. Rain hits the windows. The fire in the grate pops and settles, sending a curl of woodsmoke into the room that mingles with the wet-wool smell of Henry's coat. He smells like an airport and a law office and a man who left his umbrella in a taxi.
Then Henry Wingrave does something I don't expect.
He laughs. Low, controlled, but real. He closes the folio and sets it aside.

"You think I don't know about the Game?" He unbuttons his coat, slowly, as if he's settling in for a much longer conversation than the one he planned. "Of course I know. Peter was my employee. He designed it in my house with my children. I funded it. Not because I approved — because I couldn't stop it without destroying the family."
He says *the family* like it's a geographic feature. A mountain range. Something that was there before him and will be there after.

"You think you're the first person to try?" He meets my eyes. "Every governess threatened exposure. Every one of them took the check. Rebecca Jessel's predecessor — the Australian woman, what was her name — she threatened to go to the tabloids. She had photos. She had documentation. She had a lawyer." He pauses. "She took the check."
"I'm not every governess."

"No." Henry's voice drops, and for the first time it carries something that might be respect, or might be the careful attention of a man who has found a splinter he can use. "You're worse. You're Peter's daughter."
The room goes quiet. Even the rain seems to pause.
"You don't want to end the Game," Henry says. "You want to *win* it."

I open my mouth, but nothing comes out. Because part of me — the part I keep in the small dark room at the back of my skull where I put things I don't want to examine — knows he might be right. I came to Bly to expose the Game. But exposure is not the same as ending. Exposure is its own kind of power. Exposure is a move. And I have been playing — have been playing since the moment I walked through that door and lied about who I was.
Henry reads my silence. He nods, slowly, as if I've confirmed something.

"Let me explain how this works," he says, and now his voice has shifted into a different register — not the reasonable uncle, not the crisis manager, but something colder. Something that has made phone calls to police commissioners and hospital administrators and knows how the machinery of the state can be pointed at a single person like a fire hose. "You sign the NDA, you take the money, and you leave. That's option one. Option two: I call the police. Not the local ones — the Metropolitan Police, through channels I have used before and will use again. And I tell them the following."

He holds up a finger. "One: you entered this household under a false identity. You are not a qualified governess. You assumed the name and credentials of a relative — a dead relative, I assume, given the surname Bly — to gain access to minors."
A second finger. "Two: you have a documented psychiatric history. Involuntary commitment, if I'm not mistaken. A suicide attempt. Medication non-compliance. This is all discoverable."

My stomach turns. He knows. He knows about the institution. About the six weeks I spent in a room with a window that didn't open, talking to a therapist who smelled like peppermint and wrote everything down.

"Three." A third finger. "You have been filming minors without consent. Broadcasting them — their faces, their location, their private moments — to the internet. Two million viewers, you said. I don't imagine the courts will be impressed by your intentions."
He lets the hand drop.

"The law isn't on your side, Maeve. I'm sorry. I genuinely am. But the law protects property, and this family has more of it than you do. The law protects children from unauthorized filming, and you have done that. The law treats people with psychiatric histories as unreliable narrators, and you are that." He pauses. "You went to Bly under false pretenses. You lied about your identity. You filmed minors without consent. And you have a history that will make any judge question your credibility before you open your mouth."

I feel the walls of the room pressing in. The damp stone smell is stronger now, or maybe it's just me — the animal part of my brain recognizing a trap. The NDA means silence. The police means exposure, but for me, not them. An involuntary psych hold, probably. Back to the room with the window that doesn't open. Back to the peppermint therapist with her clipboard. Back to the fluorescent lights that buzz at a frequency that lives in your teeth.
I've been there before.
I won't go back.

"The NDA is generous," Henry says. He slides the document toward me again. The paper catches the firelight. "Seven figures. A new life. A new name, if you want one — we can arrange that. You disappear, and the story becomes 'disgruntled former employee makes baseless allegations.' The stream is already being framed that way in certain publications. Give it forty-eight hours and the public will have moved on to something else. They always do."

He's right. He is right, and the worst part is that his rightness is so *reasonable*. He isn't threatening me with violence. He isn't snarling or banging the table. He is offering me a choice between silence and destruction, and he is doing it with the calm of a man who has made this offer before and watched it work, every time, because the world is built to work this way — built for Henry Wingrave and his folio full of paper and his seven-figure solution to the problem of a woman who won't be quiet.
My hand is shaking. I press it flat against the table to stop it.

The viewer count on my phone reads 2,317,884. They are watching me consider the check. They are watching me decide whether to be bought. And some of them — the ones in the chat, the ones typing *don't sign it* and *take the money* and *he's lying* — they care, or they perform caring, but they won't be here in the morning. They will close the tab. They will go to work. They will forget Maeve Bly and the Wingrave family and the man in the greatcoat who smells like London rain and speaks like a scalpel.
I am alone in this room, no matter how many people are watching.

My fingers close around the edge of the NDA. I could sign it. I could take the money. I could walk out of Bly Manor tonight and never come back and let the Game continue under a different governess, a different name, the same rotation of horror and silence and checks that work every time because everyone is here for money, aren't they?
The paper is cold against my fingertips.
"I need a pen," I say.
Henry reaches into his coat pocket and produces one. Matte black, expensive, the kind of pen that looks like it was designed to sign things like this.
I hold it over the signature line. My hand is still shaking.
And then I hear it — footsteps on the stairs. Not the furtive, careful steps of someone trying not to be heard. Intentional steps. Deliberate. The sound of someone who wants to be noticed.
Flora appears at the top of the staircase.

She is in her nightdress, her hair loose around her shoulders, and in her arms she carries a laptop — open, awake, its screen casting blue-white light across her face. She looks like an oracle. She looks like a verdict.
She holds the laptop up, angled toward the security camera at the top of the hall.
On the screen: a video file. The thumbnail shows the lake at dawn, a long figure at the water's edge, the image grainy but unmistakable.
Miles's recording. The one Flora told me about — the one she said Miles made the night Jessel walked into the water. The one Flora said she had never seen.
She had it. She has had it the entire time.
Flora looks at me. Then at Henry. Then at the camera.
"I have evidence," she says.
Three words. They land in the room like stones dropped into still water, and the ripples are still expanding when Henry Wingrave's face goes very, very still.

The video. Miles's video. The proof that they watched — that they filmed — a woman walking into a lake to her death and did nothing. The proof that the Game did not create the horror. The horror was already there, and the Game just gave it a frame.

But it's more than proof. It's Jessel's final moments. Her last walk. Her body entering the water. Publishing this video means exposing a woman's death — her real death, not a narrative beat, not a plot point, but the actual last seconds of Rebecca Jessel's life — to two million strangers on the internet.
Flora holds the laptop like it's a grenade with the pin already pulled.

And somewhere in the control room, watching this on the feeds, Miles is seeing his sister hold the thing he never thought she had. His face — I can imagine it, though I can't see it from here. Not anger. Something worse. Resignation. The look of someone who always knew the hand would come and is tired of waiting for it to fall.
Henry Wingrave sets down his pen.

The fire crackles. The rain beats against the glass. Two million people are watching a fourteen-year-old girl hold evidence that could destroy the Wingrave family — and could violate the memory of a dead woman in the same breath.
I set the NDA on the table, unsigned.
The game isn't over.
It's just changed shape.
